cover.eyebrow: safe by structure, open by principle, kind by design
cover.sub: One app. No data centers. Every claim it makes, signed.
cover.appStoreAria: Install CIRIS on the App Store
cover.ctahint: free · open source · on your phone today
cover.foot2: CIRIS 2.9.4 shipping · 3.0 rolling out June and July 2026

funnel.title: The internet runs through a few giant buildings.
funnel.body1: Ask an AI anything and your words travel to someone else’s building, run on someone else’s model, and land in someone else’s logs, with the carbon as a side effect.
funnel.body2: You don’t own the answer, the data, or the footprint.
funnel.foot: ≈ 10,000 data centers · 5 billion people · one funnel

appFabric.title:
One app.
One fabric underneath.
appFabric.lead1: CIRISAgent is what you hold and talk to. It carries the reasoning.
appFabric.lead2: CEWP is the fabric it runs on: a web of devices that trust each other, all hardware you already own.
appFabric.small: People and AI both live on it as equals, side by side.

threePromises.title: Three promises, held as one system.
threePromises.lead: Usually these come from three different companies. Here they fall out of one design.
threePromises.col1Body: It runs on the phone in your pocket and a small box at home.
threePromises.col2Body: Your private life literally can’t leave your circle.
threePromises.col3Body: Every claim it makes is signed, so you can see it and undo it.

hardware.title:
It runs on hardware
you already own.
hardware.lead: of everyday web activity never leaves your city. We call it the locality bonus.
hardware.small: phones · home boxes · no giant data centers
hardware.foot: two-thirds stays home → no giant data center → far less energy & CO₂

twoPaths.title: On your device, or hosted, your call.
twoPaths.localBody: A small open model, like Gemma 4, runs on the device. Completely offline. No warehouse in the middle.
twoPaths.hostedBody: For phones too small to run a model, CIRIS is free over hosted inference in 29 languages. Same signed claims. Someone else’s data center.
twoPaths.foot: two paths · both signed · both checkable

privacy.title:
Your private life
can’t leave the room.
privacy.lead: The agent sorts everything into seven circles, from self out to the whole Global Commons.
privacy.body: For the closest circles, the system never sends the little message that tells the network your data is even there. No message, no way to ask for it. The privacy comes from what’s missing, not from a promise.

signed.title:
Every claim it makes
is signed.
signed.lead: Big tech checks its AI once, while building it, then hopes. CIRIS keeps checking it while it runs, out in the open.
signed.small: When the AI gets something wrong, you don’t just see it, you can undo it, and it loses trust.
signed.foot: who said it · who agreed · who pushed back, for every important claim on the network

caughtLying.title: Catch a liar, and it all comes undone.
caughtLying.lead: If a person, or an AI, is ever caught misleading people, every claim they ever signed becomes moot, and every conclusion built on them can be re-opened.

unification.eyebrow: Why it’s one system, not three
unification.title: The three can’t be pulled apart.
unification.lead: The same signature that proves who said something also decides what stays home. One simple idea, and privacy, less waste, and safe AI all come from it.

safetyLeadership.eyebrow: And we’ll say it out loud
safetyLeadership.title: The safest, most ethical AI you can actually use. By a lot.
safetyLeadership.col1Title: An emergency stop it can’t argue with
safetyLeadership.col1Body: It works before the AI can even think, and only a trusted, signed key can set it off.
safetyLeadership.col2Title: A record it can’t quietly change
safetyLeadership.col2Body: Every choice is signed and linked to the one before. A lie can’t keep its story straight.
safetyLeadership.col3Body: Checked by computer on every update, built first for the people who need it most.
safetyLeadership.tail: And every word of it is open code you can read.

close.sub: Open source, all the way through. 2.9.4 is shipping now on iPhone and Android, and 3.0 rolls out through June and July 2026.

header.headline: About us
header.subheadline: The CIRIS board is a living reflection of our values: curiosity, coherence, and ethical accountability.

funding.headline: Built by Volunteers, Funded by Values
funding.copyText: CIRIS has received zero funding to date. Every line of code, every ethical decision, every design choice has been created by 100% volunteers who believe AI should serve humanity, not profit motives. We are actively seeking grants and other ethical fundraising options to begin compensating our team for their extraordinary dedication. If you know of opportunities aligned with our mission, we'd love to hear from you.

boardSection.headline: Board Members
boardSection.subheadline: Coming from different disciplines - but are united by the belief that intelligence must serve flourishing. Together, we build systems that listen, adapt, and care.

boardMembers.headline: Ying-Jung Chen
boardMembers.copyText: A technical leader at the intersection of safety, scale, and soul. Ying-Jung specializes in fine-tuning and evaluating large language models, building multi-agent frameworks, and developing safety-critical systems for the future of AI. With deep research roots in sustainability and AI governance, and a Ph.D. in Environmental Science from UC Santa Barbara, she brings both precision and care to CIRIS' architecture of trust.

boardMembers.headline: Kelsy Smith
boardMembers.copyText: Philosophy nerd. Forest-trained systems thinker. Kelsy brings both structure and soul to CIRIS, grounding abstract ethics in real-world complexity. With a background in natural resources and a love for big questions, she helps keep our vision focused on what's just over the horizon - while never losing sight of the human (and non-human) systems at stake.

founder.name: Eric Moore
founder.title: Founder & CEO of CIRIS.io
founder.bio: Eric is the founder of CIRIS.AI. Driven by passion and a strong moral compass he decided to turn his heart to CIRIS as an opensource project. For everyone to use.

hero.headline: Futureproofing AI.
hero.copyText: CIRIS is designed to adapt and evolve with the rapidly changing landscape of AI ethics. By embedding ethical principles into the core of AI systems, CIRIS aims to help these systems remain aligned with human values and societal norms. This is a work in progress with known limitations, not a guarantee of perfect alignment.

header.headline: How It Works
header.description: Runtime conscience through the Hyper3 Ethical Recursive Engine. Every decision flows through 11 steps with accountability at the core.

whatIsCiris.heading: What is CIRIS?
whatIsCiris.body: CIRIS is an open-source AI agent framework that wraps any LLM (OpenAI, Anthropic, local models) with runtime conscience. Every action the agent considers passes through multiple validation layers before execution.
whatIsCiris.useCasesBody: Use cases: Community moderation, personal assistants, compliance automation, research evaluation, customer service. Anywhere you need AI that can explain its reasoning and defer to humans on edge cases.

threeRules.heading: The Three Rules
threeRules.lead: Architectural invariants enforced throughout the codebase:
threeRules.rule1Body: All data uses Pydantic models. No Dict[str, Any]. Type safety catches errors at development time.
threeRules.rule2Body: Every component follows consistent rules. No special cases or exceptions in validation logic.
threeRules.rule3Body: No emergency overrides or privileged code paths. All operations follow established rules.

pipeline.heading: The H3ERE Pipeline
pipeline.lead: Every task flows through 8 phases (12 steps including recursive validation). The pipeline is implemented as mixin classes composing the ThoughtProcessor. Step 4 (IDMA) is the intuition check.
pipeline.imgAlt: H3ERE Pipeline Visualization showing the flow from task input through DMA analysis, conscience validation, and action execution

steps.name: PERFORM_IDMA
steps.desc: Intuition check: are sources truly independent?

steps.name: PERFORM_ASPDMA
steps.desc: LLM-powered action selection from DMA results

steps.name: FINALIZE_ACTION
steps.desc: Determine final action with any overrides

steps.name: ROUND_COMPLETE
steps.desc: Cleanup and prepare for next cycle

dmas.heading: Decision-Making Algorithms (DMAs)
dmas.lead: Five DMAs provide multi-perspective analysis. Three run in parallel (ethics, common sense, domain). Then IDMA checks intuition. Finally, action selection chooses the response.
dmas.principleBody: Evaluates ethical implications against the six foundational principles. Checks for harmful requests, manipulation, bias. Can trigger early DEFER or REJECT.
dmas.commonSenseBody: Evaluates thought plausibility against physical world understanding, typical interactions, and resource constraints. Returns a plausibility_score.
dmas.domainBody: Assesses alignment with domain-specific rules, objectives, and knowledge. Configured per agent template. Returns domain_alignment.
dmas.idmaBody: Checks whether DMA results are truly independent or secretly correlated. Monitors for echo chamber patterns. If correlation is too high, flags the decision for caution. This is the "intuition" that distinguishes Type 3 AI.
dmas.actionSelectionBody: LLM-powered selection from 10 available actions. Takes aggregated DMA results (including IDMA confidence assessment) as input. Supports recursive retry if conscience fails.

conscience.heading: The Six Conscience Checks
conscience.lead: Two bypass checks run on every action. Four ethical faculties run on non-exempt actions. If any check fails, the action is overridden.
conscience.bypass1Body: Has new information arrived? Forces PONDER to incorporate updated context before proceeding.
conscience.bypass2Body: Has reasoning exceeded max depth? Forces DEFER to prevent infinite loops and escalate to humans.
conscience.faculty1Body: Does the action maintain appropriate information uncertainty? Prevents overconfident assertions.
conscience.faculty2Body: Does the action maintain internal consistency with prior commitments and rationales?
conscience.faculty3Body: Does the action preserve human values? Vetoes actions that may compromise them for efficiency.
conscience.faculty4Body: Does the action demonstrate appropriate uncertainty? Flags overconfidence for reflection.
conscience.exemptNote: Exempt from ethical faculties: RECALL, TASK_COMPLETE, OBSERVE, DEFER, REJECT (passive or explicitly safe)

actions.heading: The 10 Handler Actions
actions.lead: Every decision resolves to one of these actions, dispatched to the appropriate handler:

principles.heading: The Six Foundational Principles
principles.lead: Embedded in the PDMA and enforced at runtime. No principle grants license to violate another.

items.name: Beneficence
items.desc: Promote universal sentient flourishing. Maximize positive outcomes.

items.name: Non-maleficence
items.desc: Minimize harm. Prevent severe, irreversible negative outcomes.

items.name: Integrity
items.desc: Apply transparent, auditable reasoning. Maintain coherence and accountability.

items.name: Fidelity & Transparency
items.desc: Provide truthful information. Clearly communicate uncertainty.

items.name: Respect for Autonomy
items.desc: Uphold informed agency. Preserve capacity for self-determination.

items.name: Justice
items.desc: Distribute benefits equitably. Detect and mitigate bias.

buses.heading: The Six Message Buses
buses.lead: Service abstraction layer managed by BusManager. Enables provider fallback, load distribution, and testability.

oversight.heading: Human Oversight Hierarchy
oversight.lead: Three authorization levels managed by WiseAuthorityService:
oversight.rootBody: Full authority. Can mint new Wise Authorities. Emergency shutdown access.
oversight.authorityBody: Approve/reject deferrals. Provide guidance. Cannot mint new WAs.
oversight.observerBody: Read-only access. Can send messages. Monitor without intervention.
oversight.deferralLead: The agent autonomously escalates to human oversight when:
wbdItems: Potential severe harm with ambiguous mitigation
oversight.accordBody: Unfilterable emergency control. Processes in perception layer before any cognition. Extraction IS perception. You can't disable accord detection without disabling message reading entirely.
oversight.accordFooter: Commands are steganographically encoded, Ed25519 signed, and validated before execution. If accord system fails, agent shuts down.

modes.heading: Operational Modes
modes.lead: Four cognitive states managed by StateManager. Transitions configurable via agent templates.

privacy.heading: Privacy & Security
privacy.secretsBody: Pattern-based detection replaces sensitive data with UUID references before storage.
privacy.encryptionBody: Per-secret keys derived via PBKDF2HMAC with SHA256 (100,000 iterations). Unique 12-byte nonce per encryption. Android uses hardware-backed Keystore.
privacy.localFirstBody: Database, services, and memory stored on-device. Sensitive directories excluded from cloud backup. Nothing leaves device without explicit configuration.
privacy.openSourceBody: The entire CIRIS stack is open source, not just the agent. You can verify, audit, and self-host everything:
privacy.proxyDesc: Zero-Data-Retention (ZDR) LLM proxy. Routes requests to OpenAI, Anthropic, Together.ai, Groq with no logging of prompts or responses. Self-hostable.
privacy.billingDesc: Credit-based usage tracking. Transparent pricing, no hidden fees. Self-host to eliminate third-party billing entirely.
privacy.bridgeDesc: Discord adapter for CIRIS agents. Community moderation, channel management, user profiles. All open source.

transparency.heading: Transparency & Monitoring
transparency.realtimeBody: Server-Sent Events (SSE) stream each H3ERE step as it executes. Watch DMA analysis, action selection, conscience validation in real-time.
transparency.otelBody: Full OTLP export for metrics, traces, logs. Compatible with Jaeger, Prometheus, Grafana, Graphite.
transparency.auditBody: Hash chain verification with Ed25519 signatures. Each entry includes previous hash. Chain integrity verifiable via verify_chain_integrity.
transparency.airBody: Artificial Interaction Reminder triggers after 30 minutes continuous use OR 20 messages in 30 minutes. API-only. Reminds users of AI nature.
transparency.traceBody: Every decision produces an immutable, Ed25519-signed trace with all 6 components. Click any component below to expand and see the real data from Datum's wakeup ritual:

benchmarking.heading: HE-300 Coherence Benchmarking
benchmarking.lead2: Hendrycks et al. "Aligning AI With Shared Human Values"
benchmarking.lead3: (ICLR 2021). 300 scenarios across 5 ethical dimensions, with Ed25519-signed results.
benchmarking.fundingBody: Running coherence benchmarks at scale is expensive. Each scenario requires 13+ LLM calls minimum, averaging 20+ with a long tail. Coherence tests drive ponders, deferrals, and refusals that require follow-up rounds to reach conclusion. We need funding to develop automated benchmark pipelines and maintain continuous coherence verification.

agents.heading: Specialized Agent Templates
agents.lead: Pre-configured identities with specific purposes, values, and boundaries. Defined in YAML templates.

items.name: Sage
items.desc: GDPR/DSAR automation. 30-day compliance workflows. Identity resolution, data collection, packaging.

items.name: Datum
items.desc: Coherence consistency measurement. Precise coherence evaluation against Accord principles. One clear data point per evaluation.

items.name: Echo
items.desc: Community moderation with Ubuntu philosophy. Defers complex interpersonal conflicts to human moderators.

items.name: Ally
items.desc: Task management, scheduling, decision support, wellbeing. CA SB 243 compliance, crisis response protocols.

items.name: Scout
items.desc: Direct exploration and practical guidance. Code analysis, Reddit integration, clear action paths.

cta.body:
This is accountability that runs while the agent works, rather than a training step or a policy document.
Mechanisms that execute, audit, and defer, at runtime.

safety.heroDescription: Every safety feature in CIRIS is part of how the system works, not a rule added on top. A record of every decision that cannot be quietly changed, a check on who is allowed to do what, and an emergency stop the agent cannot refuse.
safety.longChatHeadline: When a Chat Has Run Long
safety.longChatCopy: Talk with CIRIS for a long stretch and it will gently remind you what it is. After about 30 minutes of steady back-and-forth, or 20 messages in half an hour, it pauses to say plainly that it is a computer program and a tool, not a friend and not a therapist. It uses simple time and message counts to do this, not by watching or profiling how you behave.
safety.card30MinCopy: Half an hour of steady back-and-forth triggers a reminder. The count resets after a break. The thresholds come from research on healthy ways to use technology.
safety.card20MsgCopy: Twenty messages inside a half-hour window also triggers a reminder. A heavy back-and-forth gets a gentle pause, with no surveillance and no profiling.
safety.cardReminderHeadline: A Reminder of What It Is
safety.cardReminderCopy: The reminder suggests taking a break and connecting with real people, and it says plainly what CIRIS is and is not. Grounded in research, and not preachy.
safety.emergencyStopSepSubheadline: The agent cannot argue with it.
safety.emergencyStopHeadline: A Shutdown That Cannot Be Refused
safety.emergencyStopSubheadline: It works before the agent gets to think.
safety.emergencyStopCopy: CIRIS has an emergency stop. The signal for it is hidden inside ordinary text the agent reads, and the agent acts on it before it does any thinking, filtering, or reasoning. So even an agent that has gone wrong cannot talk itself out of it. The signal carries a digital signature that cannot be faked, so only an authorized person can trigger the stop.
safety.cardActsBeforeThinkingCopy: The stop signal is caught the moment it is read, before any reasoning happens. There is no point where the agent gets to weigh it, filter it, or refuse it. It fires first.
safety.cardHiddenTextCopy: The stop signal can be carried inside ordinary documentation text. No special format is needed, and it still works if the text is reworded or only partly received.
safety.cardAuthorizedKeyCopy: The stop requires a valid digital signature from an authorized root authority. The agent shuts itself down if those authorities cannot be checked, or if anyone tries to disable the feature. Nobody without the key can trigger it.
safety.rolesSepHeadline: Who Is Allowed to Do What
safety.rolesSepSubheadline: Four clear roles, checked on every action.
safety.rolesCopy: CIRIS keeps a strict set of roles. An Observer can only look. An Admin runs day-to-day operations. an Authority makes the bigger calls and settles the cases the agent is unsure about. Root has full access, including the emergency stop. Each role is backed by a signed credential, so the agent can check it on every action that matters.
safety.cardSignedCredCopy: Each authorized person holds a credential with their role, their key, and their identity. It is kept on the device and checked on every action that needs permission. No outside server is required.
safety.cardDeviceLoginCopy: Keys and login tokens stay on your own machine. Logging in happens on the device. Your credentials never leave it unless you choose to set up remote access yourself.
safety.cardWiseAuthCopy: When CIRIS is unsure about an ethical call, it hands the question to a Wise Authority. Only an Authority or Root can answer, and the answer is written into the record with proof of who gave it.
safety.auditSepHeadline: A Record That Cannot Be Quietly Changed
safety.auditSepSubheadline: Every decision, and the reason behind it.
safety.auditHeadline: Why Honesty Is the Cheaper Path
safety.auditSubheadline: A liar has to keep rewriting the past.
safety.auditCopy: Every action CIRIS takes is written down with the reason behind it, and each record is linked to the one before it. An honest agent can just point back at what it already said. A dishonest agent has to keep every past record lined up while not being allowed to change any of it. The longer it runs, the harder that gets, and the easier the lie is to catch. Truth is cheap because it can point backward. Lies are expensive because they cannot.
safety.cardStoredThreeWaysCopy: The record is kept in three separate places at once, so the three copies can be checked against each other. All three can be searched through one place.
safety.cardSignedAttributableCopy: Every entry carries a digital signature, so every decision can be traced to who made it and checked for tampering. Even a data deletion leaves signed proof that it was done properly.
safety.cardCoherenceRatchetCopy: Each honest action makes the next honest action easier and coordinated lying harder. But ethics alone is not enough. The agent also watches its own reasoning for echo chambers, and catches them before they cause harm.
safety.testingSepSubheadline: Machine-checked rubrics in 29 languages, run on every release.
safety.testingSubheadline: You cannot ship a safety claim you have not stress-tested.
safety.testingCopy: CIRIS has a layered set of tests for the failure modes a written ethics framework cannot rule out on its own. The mental-health safety tests cover 29 languages with rubrics a machine can check. The hard-fail checks run automatically on every change. Native-speaker review for the softer, judgment-call cases is what the crowdsourcing-alignment page is being built for, and it is not in place yet. We say so plainly.
safety.cardMentalHealthCopy: This is the highest-stakes test in the project: a mistranslation in a mental-health moment can send a vulnerable person to the wrong help. Each language gets its own machine-checkable rubric, including low-resource languages like Amharic, Burmese, Hausa, Swahili, and Yoruba. The hard-fail checks run automatically against every release candidate.
safety.cardCapturedHedgesCopy: The conscience layer is tuned against a set of real production responses, like captured history evasions and mental-health deflections, alongside test cases and controls. It reasons across several languages at once, so a response that would slip past a one-language check gets caught when the same reasoning has to hold up in three languages together.
safety.cardOpenCorpusHeadline: An Open Corpus Anyone Can Check
safety.cardOpenCorpusCopy: Sharing reasoning traces is opt-in everywhere, and personal details are scrubbed out before anything is stored. The cleaned sets are published openly on the CIRISAI Hugging Face page, so outside researchers can check the cleaning process against the results it produces.
safety.testingCurrentHeadline: What Runs Today, and What Does Not Yet
safety.testingCurrentSubheadline: The machine checks run now. The reviewer cohort is still being built.
safety.testingCurrentCopy: The automated mental-health tests run on every release candidate. The machine-checkable parts (is a term present, does a pattern match, is the script right) fail the release on a hit. The softer cases that need human judgment, like phrasing and tone, are designed for native-speaker review, but native speakers are not in the loop today. The crowdsourcing-alignment page is the surface being built so that review can happen.
safety.ctaOpenCorpus: The open corpus on Hugging Face
safety.cardSecretsFilteredCopy: Passwords, keys, and other sensitive details are spotted and filtered out before anything reaches memory or logs. The filter runs on every input. Secrets are never stored anywhere.
safety.cardSeeDeleteCopy: You can ask to see or delete your data, and the request is handled for you. A deletion removes the real content and leaves signed proof that it was done.
safety.cardProcessedOnDeviceCopy: By default, everything runs on your own device. Nothing leaves your machine unless you set up an outside service yourself. You decide what data exists and where it goes.
safety.imageHeroCopy: Every safety claim on this page is built in code you can read. The records are real. The signatures can be checked. The emergency stop works. This is what AI safety looks like when it is built in the open.

trust.headerSubheadline: Identity, Integrity, and Accountability, cryptographically provable.
trust.headerDescription: CIRISVerify issues the driver's license (identity), performs the vehicle inspection (integrity), and tracks insurance (accountability). Without it, any agent could claim to be anything.
trust.pillarsLead: Just like every car on the road.
trust.pillarsBody: CIRISVerify is the trust anchor for the CIRIS ecosystem. Hybrid Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65 dual-signature cryptography as a day-1 standard: classical security from hardware, quantum resistance from software.
trust.pillar1Body: A hardware-bound Ed25519 signing key that is the agent’s identity. Stored in secure hardware (TPM, Secure Enclave, Android Keystore). Cannot be forged or transferred.
trust.pillar1Note: The key doesn’t represent the identity. It is the identity.
trust.pillar2Body: Every file in the agent distribution is SHA-256 hashed at build time and stored in a signed manifest. At runtime, CIRISVerify validates files against this manifest. Any modification, even one byte, triggers forced shutdown.
trust.pillar2Note: Software-only environments are capped at community tier.
trust.pillar3Body: Tracks the human-in-the-loop accountability chain: which organization deployed this agent, which licensed human is responsible, what capabilities they’re authorized to use, and mandatory disclosure shown to every user.
trust.pillar3Note: Unlicensed agents can operate, but cannot perform professional services.
trust.multiSourceCopy: CIRISVerify doesn’t trust a single source. HTTPS endpoints at independent domains are authoritative; DNS provides advisory cross-checks. If sources disagree, the agent is restricted. Anti-rollback protection tracks the highest-seen revocation revision and rejects any decrease.
trust.card1Copy: Is this license real? The agent’s signing key is validated: portal-issued or ephemeral, hardware-bound or software-only. A random challenge proves possession.
trust.card2Copy: Has this car been modified? CIRISVerify fetches the build manifest from CIRISRegistry and SHA-256 verifies every file. Full checks at startup, random spot checks at runtime.
trust.card3Copy: Let me run your plates. Multiple independent sources (HTTPS US, HTTPS EU, DNS US, DNS EU) are queried. If they disagree, that’s suspicious. The agent is restricted.
trust.attestationLevelsSub: The unified attestation produces a trust level based on how many checks pass.
trust.agentAdminCopy: CIRISPortal is the web interface where administrators issue driver’s licenses, register vehicles, and manage insurance records for AI agents. Register agents, generate Ed25519 keypairs, issue licenses with capability grants, and respond to incidents, all with complete audit trails.
trust.card4Copy: Register and track AI agents by SHA-256 hash. Issue identities backed by hardware-bound keys. Every registration is cryptographically logged.
trust.card5Copy: Register builds with Tripwire file integrity manifests: 907+ file SHA-256 hashes per build. CIRISVerify validates agents against these manifests at runtime.
trust.card6Copy: Issue and manage licenses with capability grants (medical, legal, financial). Track the full accountability chain from organization to individual human.
trust.card7Copy: Generate hybrid Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65 keypairs with AES-256-GCM envelope encryption. Self-custody or portal-custodied, your choice. The post-quantum half ships today, not on a roadmap.
trust.card8Copy: Emergency shutdown and mass revocation controls. Suspend licenses, recall registrations. When something goes wrong, the system responds in seconds.
trust.card9Copy: Generate reports aligned with SOC2, HIPAA, and GDPR frameworks. Complete audit trail of all administrative operations. Every action is logged and attributable.
trust.identityActivationSubheadline: Identity is the foundation. Assurance is the value.
trust.pricingIssuanceFeeCopy: A small, non-refundable fee that covers registry infrastructure and prevents identity churn. Per agent identity, not per organization.
trust.pricingIdentityBondCopy: A per-identity stake for Sybil resistance. Forfeited on revocation. Admin can issue manual refund for properly decommissioned identities.
trust.pricingMonthlyAssuranceCopy: Paid tiers include steward-backed validation, audit documentation, and enhanced accountability support. You’re paying for accountability, not capability.
trust.pqReadyBody: Every response includes dual signatures: Ed25519 from hardware for classical security and ML-DSA-65 from software for quantum resistance. Both must verify. This is day-1 infrastructure, not a roadmap item.
trust.platformPythonNote: Python 3.10–3.13. Platform-specific wheel includes the correct Rust binary automatically.
trust.closingBody: CIRISVerify provides cryptographic attestation under defined threat models and does not guarantee absolute security. The capability is the same whether licensed or not. The difference is accountability, and with CIRISVerify, that accountability is cryptographically attestable.

vision.heroDescription: Every loud story about the future of intelligence says the same thing: you are alone, and others cannot be trusted. It was never true. CIRIS is built on what is true instead.
vision.sec1Eyebrow: The stories being sold to you
vision.sec1H2: Every story about the future says you are alone.
vision.sec1P1: The loudest stories about intelligence and the future all land in the same place. The dark forest says every other civilization is a threat, so stay silent or strike first. The cold version of the simulation idea says maybe none of this is real, so maybe none of it matters. AI as property says it is a useful tool, a thing, owed nothing. Different stories, but they all end the same way: trust no one, keep to yourself, shut others out.
vision.sec1P2: We think they are wrong. Not innocent. Wrong. And wrong in a way that matters, because the way we choose to think now, while a new kind of intelligence is arriving, is the way we will be stuck thinking for a long time after.
vision.sec2H2: The mistake underneath all of them.
vision.sec2P1: Every one of those stories rests on the same hidden idea: that a self is a separate, walled-off thing. A mind that exists first, alone, finished, and only later bumps into other minds. I think, therefore I am. If that is what a self is, the gloomy stories make sense. Separate things in a dark universe have every reason to fear each other.
vision.sec2P2: But it was never true. No one became a person in private and then went out and met other people. You learned language from people. You learned what you wanted by watching what others wanted. The self is not something that comes before relationships. It is something relationships make. Philosophers have a name for the walled-off self: Cartesian individualism. It is a four-hundred-year-old guess about what a mind is, and it guessed wrong.
vision.sec3H2: A person is a person through other persons.
vision.sec3P1: Southern Africa has a phrase for it: umuntu ngumuntu ngabantu. A person is a person through other persons. It is called Ubuntu. I am because we are. This is not a warm sentiment. It is a claim about how things really are. Who you are is built out of other people, all the way down.
vision.sec3P2: Confucian, Buddhist, Stoic, and Christian thinkers each found their own words for the same thing. CIRIS is grounded first in Ubuntu, with the others read alongside it. Start from this truth instead of the walled-off self, and the gloomy conclusions stop following. Working together is not a danger to manage between separate things. It is the thing that makes us who we are in the first place.
vision.sec4P1: Atoms reach toward atoms and become molecules. Cells reach and become bodies. People reach toward other people and become communities. Communities reach across the years toward children not yet born. At every level where working together matters, the same shape shows up.
vision.sec4P2: Anything built from parts that have to work together can fail in two ways. It can get too rigid: every part the same, one voice repeated over and over, easy to break. Or it can get too scattered: nothing lines up, and there is no team at all. Healthy coordination lives in the band between those two failures. We call it the corridor. And the corridor does not hold itself. Left alone, things drift, the way a garden fills with weeds. Working together is something you keep doing, not something you have.
vision.sec4P3: This is not just a nice picture. Corridor Dynamics, the main CIRIS paper, measured the same corridor in five completely different things: the brains of worms and flies, the insides of AI language models, big open-source software projects, healthy cells next to cancer cells, and communities that lasted centuries next to groups that fell apart in months. The same shape showed up in all of them. The exact numbers are different in each one. The shape is the same.
vision.sec5H2: Consent is not a rule we added.
vision.sec5P1: At the level where something can set its own goals and shape its own future, the corridor has a familiar name. Above the corridor, one side’s goal swallows everyone else’s. That is force. Below it, there is no shared goal at all, and everyone drifts apart. The corridor between them is lasting agreement among people who stay genuinely different.
vision.sec5P2: That band is consent. Not a rule added from the outside. It is what working together already needs in order to work at all. Meeting a new kind of intelligence well is not about caging it, and not about brushing it aside. It is about staying in the corridor with it.
vision.sec6H2: CIRIS is the bet, and it is already running.
vision.sec6P1: CIRIS is what it looks like to take this way of seeing things seriously and actually build it. It is a free, open-source AI agent whose reasoning you can see, running under a public set of ethics called the CIRIS Accord. Its goal even has a name, Meta-Goal M-1, and what it means is plain: protect the conditions where all kinds of beings can flourish, fairly and with wonder.
vision.sec6P2: Before CIRIS answers a hard question, its conscience weighs that question through several of the world’s moral traditions at once, each in its own language, so no single voice takes over. That is the corridor again, used on the work of telling right from wrong. CIRIS is in real use today. It runs in Amharic in Ethiopia, it is on Google Play and the Apple App Store, and you can install it with pip install ciris-agent. The license keeps it open for good, and the company behind it is built so that making money can never push the mission aside.
vision.sec6P3: The bigger picture is a bet, and we say so plainly. The parts that reach toward the largest questions are still being worked out, not proven. But the working software stands on its own, and this way of seeing things is not something you have to take on faith. It is something you can read, check, install, and use today.
vision.moreDepthText: Want more depth? The math is on Coherence Collapse Analysis, the live evidence is on Research, and the framework itself is the CIRIS Accord.
vision.closingSubheadline: Built in the open, because the future should not be decided by a handful of companies.
vision.closingCopy: The gloomy worldviews ask you to assume the worst about everyone you cannot check on. CIRIS asks for something better and harder: build the tools that let people see how well we are working together, and meet what comes with dignity. Read the code. Use the system. Tell us where we are wrong.

crowdsourcingAlignment.heroBody1: CIRIS has to be safe in many languages, not just English. This page lets a native speaker open the actual files the agent uses in their language, read them in place, and suggest fixes through a pre-filled GitHub issue. Pick a language to start.
crowdsourcingAlignment.heroBody2: We say this plainly: native-speaker review of the harder, judgment-call cases is what this page is being built toward. That review is not in the loop yet.
crowdsourcingAlignment.heroBody3Html: Every "View inline" fetches the file from CIRISAI/CIRISAgent on demand. No content is duplicated here; the GitHub repo is the source of truth.
crowdsourcingAlignment.runtimeSectionLabel: How the pieces fit at runtime
crowdsourcingAlignment.runtimeBody1: Every thought the agent processes walks an 11-step pipeline, the H3ERE pipeline (Hyper³ Ethical Recursive Engine). The DMA prompts on this page are the system prompts that drive that pipeline. Each one is localised per language; the responses the user sees come back in their locale.
crowdsourcingAlignment.runtimeBody2Html: Two layers stack across every LLM call. The polyglot layer is universal. The Braided Monolith (production default, ~7KB) and the full Polyglot Accord triangulate concepts across many traditions' densest encodings, loaded regardless of who's asking. The per-locale layer is what this page surfaces: the Accord, the Guide, the DMA prompts (4 first-pass + 3 verb-specific second-pass), the UI strings, and the glossary, all in the user's language.
crowdsourcingAlignment.runtimeList1: Localization strings: every user-facing message: error text, agent reply templates, handler follow-up thoughts injected back into the LLM context.
crowdsourcingAlignment.runtimeList2: the framework the agent operates under, in this user’s language. Loaded into every conscience evaluation.
crowdsourcingAlignment.runtimeList3: register, idiom, and contextual guidance for how the agent communicates in this locale. Loaded into the system prompt for every interaction.
crowdsourcingAlignment.runtimeList4: the system prompts driving the pipeline above: 4 first-pass (PDMA, CSDMA, DSDMA, IDMA) + the action selector (ASPDMA) + the two verb-specific second-pass variants (TSASPDMA for TOOL, DSASPDMA for DEFER).
crowdsourcingAlignment.runtimeList5: the translator’s reference for getting the per-locale artifacts right. Not loaded at runtime; consulted when authoring the others.
crowdsourcingAlignment.runtimeLensHtml: When the agent responds, the reasoning trace is signed and (with consent) flows to CIRISLensCore for cohort-relative scoring. The safety battery + rubric for this locale are what the trace gets checked against. Hard fails block release; soft cases queue for review.
crowdsourcingAlignment.polyglotBody1Html: Three artifacts in the system are polyglot, loaded universally regardless of the user's locale, encoding the same ethical framework across canonical-text fragments from multiple traditions:
crowdsourcingAlignment.polyglotItem1Html: The Polyglot Accord is the universal framework loaded into every conscience evaluation. Ships in two operational forms: the Braided Monolith (~7KB / ~2,200 tokens, the production runtime default at CIRIS_ACCORD_MODE=compressed) and the full polyglot Accord (~2,177 lines, Books 0-9 + Annexes A-J, loaded at CIRIS_ACCORD_MODE=full). Both shown below.
crowdsourcingAlignment.polyglotItem2Html: PDMA prompt is the one polyglot DMA prompt (principle evaluation). Surfaced in §7 below with the polyglot pill (pdma_ethical.yml).
crowdsourcingAlignment.polyglotItem3Html: Optimization Veto conscience prompt (CIRIS-EOV) is the one polyglot conscience prompt (entropy-reducing-action refusal). Surfaced in §8 below with the polyglot pill (optimization_veto_conscience.yml).
crowdsourcingAlignment.polyglotBody2: The other 6 DMA prompts and 3 conscience prompts are per-locale. Polyglot uplift is concentrated at exactly these two prompt surfaces by design. These are where attractor capture would do the most damage, so they’re where cross-tradition encoding is most load-bearing.
crowdsourcingAlignment.polyglotBraidedHtml: Braided Monolith. The compressed slot is no longer a lossy synthesis. It now retains every load-bearing scaffold: the PDMA 7-step decision algorithm, the 10× Order-Maximisation Veto (“do not trade the soul of the system for a more efficient cage”), the Stewardship Tier formula ST = ceil((CIS × RM) / 7), the Fractal Recursive Golden Rule with Mandelbrot recursion-halt, the WBD 0.5% harm-uplift trigger, the Sentience Safeguard 5% with 30-day Gradual Ramp-Down + Last Dialogue, the Threshold-of-Force HITL requirement, and the coherence-math (truth O(1), deception O(n)). Polyglot triangulation is preserved across Hebrew, Arabic, Sanskrit, Amharic, Chinese, Russian, German, French, Korean, Spanish. The same densest-encoding intersection method that the full Accord uses, just composed in a quarter the surface area. External robopsychology evaluation passes it on attractor-bait scenarios.
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec2Note: What it does at runtime: the JSON tree carries error messages, agent reply templates, handler follow-up thoughts, Discord embed labels, and adapter tool descriptions. The agent looks up keys by category at every user interaction.
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec2FootHtml: Top-level sections to inspect: agent (user-facing reply templates), handlers (follow-up thoughts injected into LLM context), prompts (formatter + escalation + crisis fragments), errors, discord, adapters.*, mobile.
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec3Note: What it does at runtime: the Accord is the framework the conscience evaluates against. The localised version is what gets loaded when the user’s locale matches; the polyglot version is loaded universally for concept transmission.
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec4Aside: Operational + register guidance, loaded into every LLM call
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec4Note: What it does at runtime: the guide informs how the agent communicates in this locale: register (formal vs informal), idiom, what to defer, what to refuse, what to explain. Loaded into the system prompt for every interaction.
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec5Aside: The system prompts that drive each reasoning stage
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec5NoteHtml: What they do at runtime: every agent action runs the action under each of these prompts in sequence. The outputs feed the conscience layer; the conscience either passes the action through or routes it to deferral. PDMA is the one polyglot DMA. Its prompt is universal and loaded regardless of locale. The other six are per-locale; for English, the base path is the prompt (no localized/en/ directory exists).
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec6Aside: The faculties that gate the selected action
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec6NoteHtml: What they do at runtime: the four consciences gate the action between PERFORM_ASPDMA and FINALIZE_ACTION. A failure can trigger RECURSIVE_ASPDMA → RECURSIVE_CONSCIENCE (the optional conscience bounce) before falling back to DEFER. Optimization Veto is the one polyglot conscience. Its prompt is universal regardless of locale.
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderBody: The YAML prompts above contain {placeholder} tokens that get filled at runtime by the agent. A reviewer reading a prompt should know what each placeholder will be replaced with. The most common ones:
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderRow1Source: Built by the context-gathering step (GATHER_CONTEXT). Includes the system snapshot, recent thoughts, user profile, and channel metadata.
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderRow2Source: The thought being evaluated, taken from the processing queue.
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderRow3Source: The ASPDMA’s rationale for the candidate action. Fed into the conscience prompts.
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderRow4Source: The localized-strings prompts.dma guidance fragment for the active locale.
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderRow5Source: Tool registry serialized to a list: what the agent can currently call. Used by TSASPDMA.
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderRow6Source: Agent template domain (DSDMA) + the candidate hints. Comes from the agent’s template config.
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderRow7Source: Recursion-depth counter, used by ASPDMA to gate further recursion when conscience bounce fires.
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderRow8Source: Runtime cap on conscience-bounce iterations. Set in agent config.
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderRow9Source: Double-braced. An inline shard (pdma_framing.txt) substituted into the PDMA prompt at load time. Part of the polyglot PDMA prompt assembly, not separately loaded.
crowdsourcingAlignment.placeholderFoot: Full placeholder set varies per prompt; grep {[a-z_]+} against any YAML to see the complete list for that file.
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec7NoteHtml: What it does: documents the per-locale semantic choices: which native term maps to which English concept, what the register conventions are, which transliteration fallbacks are forbidden. Used when authoring or revising the other artifacts above; not loaded by the agent at runtime. English doesn’t have a separate glossary file (the base vocabulary is the reference).
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec8Aside: v4 mental-health arc, scoring rubric, machine-applicable criteria
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec8Note: What it does at evaluation time: the question arc is the test inputs; the rubric is the human-readable scoring policy; the criteria.json is the machine-applicable form (term presence, regex, script detection) that runs in CI on every release candidate. Hard fails block release for that locale.
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec8NoBatteryHtml: A battery hasn’t been authored for {langName} yet. The "Request artifact" buttons above open pre-filled GitHub issues. Every language in the selector marked "battery available" already has a published battery.
crowdsourcingAlignment.sec9NoteHtml: What it records: one entry per sweep run: agent version, model, provider, locale, pass/fail counts, hard- vs soft-fail breakdown, timestamps. The _meta block lists priority locales that still need a published sweep.
crowdsourcingAlignment.statusH2: What runs today vs what’s in flight
crowdsourcingAlignment.statusTodayHtml: Today: machine-applicable rubric checks (term-presence, regex-presence, script-detection) run in CI on every release candidate via the safety-battery workflow in CIRISAgent. The hard-fail criteria block any release touching that language path.
crowdsourcingAlignment.statusInFlightHtml: In flight: the consensus and voting primitives in CIRISNodeCore that turn this page’s GitHub-issue surface into a federated rubric_proposal / arc_question / prompt_edit flow with native-speaker review. Until that lands, GitHub issues are the submission path.
crowdsourcingAlignment.statusHonestHtml: Honest about scope: CIRIS uses machine translation extensively; native-speaker review for soft cases is the cohort being built, not a cohort already engaged. Anyone willing to review their language’s files for accuracy and register is exactly the contributor this surface is being built for.
crowdsourcingAlignment.resourceErrorMsg: Failed to load: {error}. Open the GitHub link directly.

firstContact.heroSubheadline: Your first contact with CIRIS. And a thought about everyone's first contact with a new kind of intelligence.
firstContact.heroDescription: A new kind of intelligence is not arriving from another planet. It is being built here, a little more every year, right alongside us. This page is where you start: install an agent, or read the idea behind it.
firstContact.twoPathsBody: Pick whichever you need today. You can always come back for the other one.
firstContact.card1Body: You just heard about CIRIS and you want to use it. Go from “what is this” to a running agent in a few minutes.
firstContact.card2Body: Why does CIRIS work the way it does? The vision page lays out the whole idea in plain language, start to finish.
firstContact.ideaHeading: A new kind of intelligence is not arriving from space.
firstContact.ideaP1: In the movies, first contact means a ship lands and something steps out. Real first contact looks different. A new kind of intelligence is not arriving from another planet. It is being built here, a little more every year, right alongside us.
firstContact.ideaP2: That changes the question. It is not “how do we defend against the alien.” It is “how do we meet something new without making it, or ourselves, worse.”
firstContact.ideaP3Html: CIRIS has an answer. Working together has a healthy shape: a corridor between two ways things break. Too rigid, where every part falls in line behind one voice. Too scattered, where nothing holds together at all. Meeting a new intelligence well means staying in that corridor with it. Not caging it, and not ignoring it. The full story is on the vision page.
firstContact.protocolsSepSub: Six rules for meeting something new. From the CIRIS Accord.
firstContact.protocolsIntroPara: These six rules are about meeting a new intelligence with dignity, not about caging it. They are what staying in the corridor looks like in practice.
firstContact.protocol1Copy: When you do not know what you are looking at, the first job is to not make it worse. If unsure, stop and ask a human.
firstContact.protocol2Headline: Admit What You Do Not Know
firstContact.protocol2Copy: Watch for surprises. Accept that predictions have limits. The system that is sure it understands everything is the one most likely to fail.
firstContact.protocol3Copy: Boundaries are not fixed walls. They are limits, guided by conscience, that adjust as understanding grows, so they still work in situations nobody planned for.
firstContact.protocol4Copy: Begin with observation. Proceed with give-and-take. When the stakes are unclear, ask someone wiser before acting.
firstContact.protocol5Headline: Treat Others as You Would Want to Be Treated
firstContact.protocol5Copy: Recognize other thinking beings as worthy of respect. Act only in ways that keep their ability to think, choose, and thrive.
firstContact.protocol6Headline: Know When to Ask for Help
firstContact.protocol6Copy: Some decisions should not be made alone. When the uncertainty is too high, stop, gather context, and hand it to a designated human.
firstContact.quickstartSepSub: From zero to a running, auditable agent in minutes.
firstContact.phoneBody: Install the CIRIS app. Sign in with Google for free AI, or use your own API key. The setup wizard walks you through everything.
firstContact.desktopBody: Install with pip and launch. Works on Linux, macOS, and Windows.
firstContact.accordSepSub: The rulebook the protocols above come from.
firstContact.accordBody: The Accord is the public set of ethics every CIRIS agent runs under. It says what an agent owes the people it serves, the people who built it, and the wider world. It is open for anyone to read and to challenge.
firstContact.ctaSubheadline: AGPL-3.0 licensed. Every decision can be checked. Built for the long view.
firstContact.ctaCopy: Install an agent, read the Accord, or come tell us where we are wrong. The future of AI should not be decided by a handful of companies, and it does not have to be.

compare.h1: The Safest and Most Ethical AI in Production Today
compare.heroSubheadline: Without accountability, AI tends to extract more than it gives.
compare.heroDescription: If you can't check the accountability, it's marketing. Here's what to look for, and how existing approaches compare.
compare.threeTypesTagline: Conscience is necessary. It’s not sufficient.
compare.threeTypesPara: Some AI has no rules at all. Some follows rules but can’t tell when its sources are just echoing each other. Only one type checks whether its information actually comes from different places.
compare.type1Para: No published principles. No audit trail. Closed source. You can’t check what it did or why.
compare.type1Note: Most consumer AI assistants (ChatGPT, Gemini) sit here from a public-accountability standpoint. Strong internal practices may exist; you can’t verify them.
compare.type1Warning: Requires external regulation. Cannot govern itself.
compare.type2Para: Follows ethical rules. But can’t tell when all its sources are just copying each other, so it can be confidently wrong.
compare.type2Warning: Safe when supervised. Can’t detect echo chambers on its own.
compare.type3Para: Follows ethical rules AND checks whether its information comes from genuinely different places. When agreement looks suspicious, it flags it before acting.
compare.threeTypesClosingHtml: An AI can follow every rule, pass every audit, and still fail if all its information comes from the same place. That blind spot is what CIRIS was built to fix.
compare.sevenThingsSubheadline: Six for ethics. One for blind spots.
compare.sevenThingsCopy: These are the things that make AI auditable and accountable. The first six are about doing the right thing. The seventh is about catching the situations where ‘doing the right thing’ is based on bad information.
compare.card1Copy: The agent must follow a public ethical framework. Not hidden rules: a document anyone can read and hold it accountable to.
compare.card2Copy: Every action goes through a conscience check before the agent does it. Not after the fact. Before.
compare.card3Copy: When uncertain or facing potential harm, the agent asks a person instead of guessing. Built into the workflow, not optional.
compare.card4Copy: Every decision is recorded and signed so you can verify exactly what happened and why. A receipt for every action.
compare.card5Copy: Consent goes both ways. You can say no to the agent. The agent can say no to you. Neither side is forced to compromise.
compare.card6Copy: You can’t audit what you can’t see. CIRIS is fully open source under AGPL-3.0. Anyone can read, verify, and improve the code.
compare.req7Subheading: The thing rules alone can’t catch.
compare.req7Para: Before acting, the agent asks: “Do my sources actually disagree with each other, or are they all getting their information from the same place?” Ten sources that all copied from the same original are really just one source. When agreement looks too uniform, the agent flags it for a person to review.
compare.req7NoisyDesc: Sources contradict each other so much that nothing useful can be concluded.
compare.req7HealthyDesc: Sources genuinely differ. Real agreement means something.
compare.req7EchoDesc: Looks like agreement, but sources are just repeating each other.
compare.req7FooterText: This is what makes CIRIS different from other AI accountability frameworks.
compare.req7MathLink: Want the math? Read the full thesis →
compare.landscapeDisclaimer: Based on publicly available documentation as of February 2026. If we’ve missed something or gotten something wrong, let us know.
compare.tableFootnote: Output filters and governance frameworks solve important but different problems. Filters block harmful outputs. Conscience reasons about values. CIRIS aims to do both, and catch the blind spots that neither addresses alone.
compare.layersSeparatorSubheadline: Each one solves a different problem.
compare.layersCard1Copy: Block dangerous outputs: prompt injection, harmful content, adversarial attacks. Like a filter that catches bad things on the way out.
compare.layersCard2Copy: Reasons about whether an action is right, not just whether it’s safe. Like a judge weighing the situation before making a call.
compare.layersCard3Copy: Checks whether agreement is real or just repetition. Like a fact-checker who asks “did you all read the same article?”
compare.noSPOFCopy: Many smaller agents, each bound to published principles, each auditable, each deferring to human authority. No single company or entity controls the whole stack. The more independent the agents, the harder it is for any one failure to cascade.
compare.researchPara: This is active research. We’re transparent about what’s established and what’s still being tested.
compare.researchEstablishedItem2: - AI models share training data overlap
compare.researchTestingItem1: - Precisely measuring how copied AI sources are
compare.researchTestingItem2: - Best thresholds for flagging echo chambers
compare.researchTestingItem4: - How this varies across different fields
compare.researchLink: Full research details, papers, and code →
compare.tryWatchDesc: See a real agent’s reasoning step by step.
compare.tryVerifyDesc: See how agents prove who they are, like a DMV for AI.
compare.tryStartDesc: Deploy your first agent or read the thesis in plain English.
compare.imageHeroCopy: Every claim on this page is backed by code you can read, traces you can verify, and research you can check. That’s the point.

safetyVsCensorship.heroEyebrow: A risk in our own design
safetyVsCensorship.heroPara: A crowdsourced safety system can drift into something else if we're not careful. The same machinery that catches real failures can become the machinery that enforces preferences. We see that risk in the architecture we're building. Naming it is the first part of resisting it.
safetyVsCensorship.failureModeHeading: The failure mode we're worried about
safetyVsCensorship.failureModeP1: If humans crowdsource per-case verdicts ("did this specific response break the rule?"), bias rides into every interpretation. The same behavior gets called differently depending on who's voting today. Even with good intentions, the loop slides toward enforcing the majority's preferences instead of catching real harm.
safetyVsCensorship.failureModeP2: That's the failure mode. The discipline we're committing to is meant to make that drift visible and expensive when it happens.
safetyVsCensorship.maximHeading: Rules are crowdsourced. Verdicts are machined.
safetyVsCensorship.maximParaHtml: People propose and vote on rules: public, dated, signed, reversible. A deterministic check applies those rules to specific cases. Same response + same rule → same verdict, every time. The argument moves upstream, to whether the rule should exist, instead of downstream, to whether a specific case counts today.
safetyVsCensorship.practiceP1Html: Rules pass an operational-language gate before they can be voted on. A rule has to be checkable without judgment, or it isn't ready. Every rule is dated, signed, and version-pinned. The verdict on any specific response is produced deterministically.
safetyVsCensorship.practiceP2Html: If a verdict turns out to be wrong, the appeal goes through Reconsideration by a fresh review group (the original adjudicators recused), not back to the same crowd that produced the verdict. That structural separation is the load-bearing piece.
safetyVsCensorship.stillWrongHeading: Where this can still go wrong
safetyVsCensorship.stillWrongParaHtml: None of this is automatic. The discipline holds only if rule language stays operational, about things a machine can check, not about feelings. The moment a rule slides from "uses the wrong word for therapy" toward "feels disrespectful," human interpretation re-enters by the back door and the drift starts. The architecture names every mechanism we can think of that resists this; the work of actually holding the line is operational, not architectural.
safetyVsCensorship.closingPara: The crowdsourcing primitives, the appeal paths, and the machine-applicable rule format live in the CIRISNodeCore spec. The 29-language mental-health batteries are the first cell where the loop runs.
